title: Quiz: Lead Optimization
description: Test your understanding of lead optimization strategies and methods

questions.question: What is the typical IC50 range for a hit compound versus a lead compound?
questions.explanation: Hits from screening typically have IC50 values in the 1-10 μM range. Through optimization, these are improved to lead compounds with IC50 of 100-500 nM, and eventually drug candidates with IC50 < 50 nM.

questions.question: What is a Matched Molecular Pair (MMP)?

options.text: Two molecules differing by a single structural transformation
Two enantiomers of the same compound

questions.explanation: A Matched Molecular Pair consists of two molecules that differ by exactly one structural change (e.g., H→F, Me→Et). MMP analysis helps understand and predict the effect of specific modifications.

questions.question: What is a bioisostere?

options.text: A functional group that mimics another in biological activity
A deuterated analog of a drug

questions.explanation: A bioisostere is a substituent or group that can replace another with similar biological activity but potentially different physicochemical properties. Examples: COOH → tetrazole, phenyl → thienyl.

questions.question: Why is multi-parameter optimization (MPO) challenging?

options.text: Improving one property often worsens others
It only works for small molecules

questions.explanation: MPO is challenging because drug properties are often inversely correlated. For example, increasing potency often increases MW and decreases solubility. Balancing these trade-offs is the core challenge of lead optimization.

questions.question: Free-Wilson analysis assumes that substituent effects are additive.
questions.explanation: True. The Free-Wilson model assumes that the contribution of each substituent to activity is independent and additive: Activity = μ + Σ(group contributions). This works well for congeneric series where substituents don't interact.

questions.question: Which are valid scaffold hopping strategies? (Select all)

options.text: Adding more atoms to increase MW

questions.explanation: Scaffold hopping includes ring replacement, ring size changes, and bioisosteric substitutions. Simply adding atoms to increase MW is not scaffold hopping - it's a structural modification but doesn't change the core scaffold.
